texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the lightest metal, lithium has a range of attributes such as high energy density by weight and high electrochemical potential (3.045 V) make this element useful for many applications, including rechargeable lithium ion batteries (LIBs), ceramics, greases, glass, primary aluminum production, polymers and so on [1]. Today LIBs represent about 37% of the rechargeable battery world market and their use is increasing, thereby increasing the need for lithium [2,3]. Due to the tremendous growth in the demand for LIBs used in hybrid and electric vehicles, a lithium shortage may emerge in a few decades [4 -6]. Lithium is usually extracted from brine [7 -15] and mineral [16,17], while ignoring other resources. The rising demand for lithium calls for prospecting and processing all viable resources especially the secondary resources. In fact, the amount of lithium (5 -7 wt%) present in the spent LIBs is signi fi cant, and therefore, the spent LIBs have been identi fi ed as one of the potential resources for lithium production in near future [18].

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At present, the projected demand for lithium calls for processing all viable resources especially secondary resources. This paper presents a promising approach for recovering lithium from low lithium high-salt solution which is usually produced in the spent LIBs recycling process. The solution was fi rstly puri fi ed, and then lithium was precipitated by phosphate, the e ff ects of operating conditions on the Li3PO4 precipitation behaviors were evaluated. The results indicated that temperature is a more important factor than seed crystal or fl occulant. After that, Li3PO4 was dissolved by acid as the anolyte, and electrodialysis with cation-exchange membranes was used to investigate the separation performance of Li and P. The results showed that Li and P was e ff ectively separated by electrodialysis, and the P/Li mass ratio of the catholyte was reduced to 0.23 (6.5 times lower compared to a feed P/Li ratio of 1.48). Deep separation of lithium and phosphorus in the catholyte was achieved by raising pH to make the lithium precipitated with the permeated phosphorus. The lithium concentration of puri fi ed catholyte was 22.5 g/L and was used to prepare lithium carbonate. The Li2CO3 precipitation rate reached 88.3% at 80 °C under CO3 3 -/Li + molar ratio of 1.1:2. The fi nal product of lithium carbonate was in accord with the standard speci fi cation (Li2CO3-0, GB/T 11075-2013). The results of this study provide an e ffi cient and green cyclic process for recovering lithium from spent LIBs.

texts.orig: After recycling the spent LIBs, usually a low lithium high-salt solution containing other metal impurities is obtained [22,23]. texts.orig: More importantly, until recently, there is little information available in literature about subsequent processing of the Li3PO4 precipitation. The traditional method is to roast lithium phosphate with calcium oxide and then the agglomerate was leached with water, thus lithium phosphate converted to lithium hydroxide [28]. However, the high reaction temperature in the process inevitably leads to high energy consumption. Not only that, the concentration of obtained LiOH is low, and the recovery e ffi ciency of lithium is not high. Furthermore, Li [29] reported method in which the lithium phosphate was fi rstly dissolved by acid, and then calcium salt was used to precipitate phosphorus to obtain lithium chloride solution. However, extra calcium needs to be further removed, and amount of residue also lead to the loss of lithium more than 15%. Similarly, Hu [30] used iron salts to remove phosphorus which also exist this problem. Therefore, it is urgent to exploit technoeconomically feasible, environment friendly and sustainable method to process Li3PO4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As we all know, electrodialysis is a ' no phase transition ' process, thus, the energy consumption of electrodialysis is low [31]. Electrodialysis is an electrochemical membrane separation processes in which the electrical fi eld induced transports anions or cations through ion conductive membranes positioned between the electrodes. Under the in fl uence of electrical potential di ff erence, the result is an enrichment of certain ions in the concentrate stream and a depletion of certain ions in the dilute stream [32]. Therefore, electrodialysis has excellent selectivity for separating counter-ions and co-ions. Nowadays, electrodialysis and its related technologies have been widely used in industrial wastewater treatment and brackish water desalination [33 -37]. Therefore, it can be assumed that if Li3PO4 is dissolved by acid, the lithium cations and phosphorus anions may be separated by electrodialysis. Dissolving Li3PO4 as anolyte, under the e ff ect of electrodialysis with cation exchange membranes, the cathode chamber may obtain high concentration LiOH which can be directly prepared for Li2CO3 product, and meanwhile, the generated acid in anode chamber can be used to dissolve the subsequent Li3PO4 raw materials. It is hoped that the question will be resolved with our proposed method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The impurity metal ions in the typical mixture solution can be precipitated by OH -, while lithium cannot. The mass ratio of impurity metal ions and lithium ion Me/Li is less than 2. As we know, in the process of lithium extraction from brines, the precipitation technique can e ff ectively separate Li and Mg, when the Mg/Li mass ratio is less than 6 [38]. So the typical mixture solution can be seen as high quality salt lake, the solution may be e ff ectively puri fi ed by adjusting the solution pH. As shown in Fig. 2 the precipitation ratio of metal impurities increased with pH, while that of Li did not change basically. When the pH increased to 5.3, Al 3+ , Fe 2+ , Cu 2+ ions were deposited quickly, and a large amount of Zn 2+ , Ni 2+ , Co 2+ ions were deposited as the pH increased to 7, and Mn 2+ , Ca 2+ , Mg 2+ ions were also precipitated completely with pH rose to 12. The impurity metal ions were removed e ff ectively (about 99%) and a low level loss of Li (4%) was obtained, when the solution pH value was maintained around 12. The solution precipitated under di ff erent experiment conditions were shown in Table 3. The results demonstrate it is a high e ffi cient and stable impurity removal method.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3.1. Separation of Li and P by electrodialysis
texts.text: 3.3.1. Separation of Li and P by electrodialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to transform the lithium phosphate into lithium carbonate, Li and P was separated by electrodialysis fi rstly. Li3PO4 solid was dissolved by acid to obtain initial anolyte (100 ml), the initial concentrations of Li and P were 43 and 64 g/L, and NaNO3 solution (40 g/L) was used as the catholyte (50 ml). The Li and P concentrations of both the anolyte and catholyte were determined at 6, 10, 13, 15.5 and 20 h of the electrodialysis reaction. In the electrolytic cell, oxygen was released around anode led to the decrease of anions OH -in anolyte, at the same time, hydrogen was released around cathode led to the decrease of cations H + in catholyte. To maintain the electrical neutrality in the solution, plenty of Li + ions in anode chamber were required to migrate through the cation-exchange membrane into the cathode chamber. While a major portion of phosphorus anions were prevented by the cation-exchange membrane and retained in anode chamber, thus realized the purpose of Li and P separation.
texts.text: In order to transform the lithium phosphate into lithium carbonate, Li and P was separated by electrodialysis fi rstly. Li3PO4 solid was dissolved by acid to obtain initial anolyte (100 ml), the initial concentrations of Li and P were 43 and 64 g/L, and NaNO3 solution (40 g/L) was used as the catholyte (50 ml). The Li and P concentrations of both the anolyte and catholyte were determined at 6, 10, 13, 15.5 and 20 h of the electrodialysis reaction. In the electrolytic cell, oxygen was released around anode led to the decrease of anions OH -in anolyte, at the same time, hydrogen was released around cathode led to the decrease of cations H + in catholyte. To maintain the electrical neutrality in the solution, plenty of Li + ions in anode chamber were required to migrate through the cation-exchange membrane into the cathode chamber. While a major portion of phosphorus anions were prevented by the cation-exchange membrane and retained in anode chamber, thus realized the purpose of Li and P separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The curves of the lithium and phosphorus concentration versus electric charge in electrolyte are shown in Fig. 6. The lithium concentration of cathode chamber increased almost linearly with the electrolytic process, correspondingly, the lithium concentration of anode chamber decreased linearly. With the extension of electrolysis time, the phosphorus anions also permeated slowly into the cathode chamber, resulting in the slowly decreased phosphorus concentration in anolyte and gradually increased phosphorus concentration in catholyte. Moreover, the migration of lithium with electrolytic time showed a more remarkable uptrend as compared to the leak rate of phosphorus, thus achieved the separation of Li and P. In the process of electrodialysis, H2SO4 was added into the catholyte intermittently to maintain the pH value less than 4 to avoid the formation of Li3PO4 by the permeated lithium and phosphorus ions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The plot of the current e ffi ciency as a function of electric charge is shown in Fig. 7. The lithium ions in anode chamber gradually migrated into the cathode chamber and the concentration decreased with electrolysis process. Simultaneously, the generated increased H + ions were also migrated across the membrane to cathode chamber, which resulted in a gradually reduced current e ffi ciency. Even so, the current e ffi ciency was also kept more than 50% in the whole process of electrodialysis. When the electric charge reached 12 A h, namely reacted 20 h, the lithium and phosphorus concentration in cathode chamber reached 30.8 g/L and 7.2 g/L, respectivly. Considering the current e ffi ciency and Li, P concentration, the electrolysis process was terminated at 12 A h. The average current was 0.6 A and current density reached relatively high value 184 A/m 2 , and the speci fi c energy consumption was only 0.027 kWh/g Li + , the order of magnitudes was same with literatures [31,39]. The mass ratio of P/Li was sharply decreased from 1.48 (initial anolyte) to 0.23 (catholyte), and the interception rate of P was 95%, lithium and phosphorus was preliminarily separated by electrodialysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The catholyte obtained after electrodialysis need to be further puri fi ed. Deep separation of lithium and phosphorus was achieved by raising the catholyte pH to make the lithium precipitated with the permeated phosphorus. The precipitate Li3PO4 can be dissolved with H + which generated from the anolyte in the electrodialysis process, and then new anolyte contain high concentration lithium was obtained. This way make the electrolytic products circulated and do not introduce other impurities.

The precipitate Li3PO4 can be dissolved with H + which generated from the anolyte in the electrodialysis process, and then new anolyte contain high concentration lithium was obtained. This way make the electrolytic products circulated and do not introduce other impurities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pH of the catholyte was adjusted by NaOH, experiments were carried out at 60 °C for 2 h with a stirring speed of 500 r/min. The catholyte turned into milky white turbid liquid when the pH value rose to 4.5, and a large number of white precipitate appeared when continued to raise the pH value to 11. Fig. 8 showed the XRD and SEM micrographs of the precipitates. It can be seen that the di ff raction lines of the precipitated solid correspond to Li3PO4 (JCPDS 25-1030). Fig. 8(b) showed that the precipitate Li3PO4 had an olivine structure with a diagonal length between 0.5 and 1 μ mwhich is within the range reported by others [40]. Compared with the Li3PO4 in Fig. 5, the size of the solid particles in Fig. 8 was smaller obviously. This is because the lithium and phosphorus ions are coexist in the solution and when OH -ions were added into the solution, lithium and phosphorus ions formed a large number of original Li3PO4 crystal nucleus. The nucleation speed is greater than the crystal growth speed, which led to the smaller size of the fi nal precipitation particle. The lithium concentration of puri fi ed catholyte was 22.5 g/L, and phosphorus concentration was less than 0.02 g/L. The deep separation of Li and P was achieved, and Li3PO4 precipitate was returned back to anode chamber to prepare anolyte, puri fi ed catholyte was used in next step.
